experiments.name: No Screens Before Bed
experiments.description: No screens for 60 minutes before bed
experiments.evidence_summary: Blue light suppresses melatonin production by up to 50%. Screen-free wind-down improves sleep onset latency.
experiments.evidence_citation: Chang et al., PNAS 2015
experiments.hypothesis_template: Eliminating screens 60 minutes before bed for {duration} days will reduce subjective sleep onset latency and improve sleep consistency.
experiments.protocol_template: No phones, tablets, laptops, or TV after 9pm (or 60 min before target bedtime). Replace with reading, journaling, stretching, or conversation. Track compliance daily.
experiments.why_it_matters: The screen is the last thing most people see before sleep and the first thing they see after. Breaking that loop is the simplest sleep intervention that exists.

experiments.name: Fixed Wake Time
experiments.description: Wake within ±15 minutes of the same time every day, including weekends
experiments.evidence_summary: Consistent wake times anchor the circadian clock. Social jet lag (weekend sleep-ins) disrupts metabolic and hormonal rhythms.
experiments.evidence_citation: Wittmann et al., Chronobiology International 2006
experiments.hypothesis_template: Waking within ±15 min of {target_time} for {duration} days will improve average HRV by ≥5% and sleep score by ≥3 points vs baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: Set alarm for same time every day including weekends. No snooze. Log actual wake time. Measure HRV, sleep score, RHR daily via Whoop.
experiments.why_it_matters: Your body doesn't know it's Saturday. The most powerful sleep hack isn't a supplement — it's consistency.

experiments.name: Cold Bedroom Protocol
experiments.description: Sleep environment at 65°F / 18°C using Eight Sleep or thermostat
experiments.evidence_summary: Core body temperature drop of 1-2°F is required for sleep onset. Cool environments increase deep sleep percentage.
experiments.evidence_citation: Harding et al., Current Biology 2019
experiments.hypothesis_template: Sleeping at 65°F for {duration} days will increase deep sleep percentage by ≥10% vs baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: Set Eight Sleep to -2 or lower. Bedroom thermostat to 65°F. Consistent bedding. Track deep sleep %, total sleep, HRV via Whoop.
experiments.why_it_matters: Most people sleep in rooms that are too warm. A $0 thermostat adjustment can outperform most sleep supplements.

experiments.name: Morning Sunlight Exposure
experiments.description: Get direct sunlight within 30 minutes of waking for 10+ minutes
experiments.evidence_summary: Morning light exposure advances the circadian clock and suppresses melatonin, improving alertness and next-night sleep onset.
experiments.evidence_citation: Blume et al., Sleep Medicine Reviews 2019
experiments.hypothesis_template: 10 minutes of morning sunlight within 30 min of waking for {duration} days will improve sleep onset consistency.
experiments.protocol_template: Go outside within 30 min of waking. No sunglasses. Face general direction of sun (not direct stare). 10 min minimum. Overcast days still count (10,000+ lux). Track compliance and sleep onset.
experiments.why_it_matters: Your eyes set your clock. The Pacific Northwest makes this harder — which makes it more important.

experiments.name: Magnesium Glycinate Before Bed
experiments.description: 400mg magnesium glycinate 30-60 minutes before bed
experiments.evidence_summary: Magnesium supports GABA activity and muscle relaxation. Glycinate form has superior bioavailability and calming effect.
experiments.evidence_citation: Abbasi et al., J Res Med Sci 2012
experiments.hypothesis_template: 400mg magnesium glycinate before bed for {duration} days will increase deep sleep % by ≥8% vs baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: Take 400mg magnesium glycinate 30-60 min before target bedtime. No other supplement changes. Track deep sleep %, sleep score, HRV via Whoop.
experiments.why_it_matters: Most adults are magnesium-deficient. This is one of the best-studied, lowest-risk sleep supplements.

experiments.name: No Caffeine After 12pm
experiments.description: Zero caffeine consumption after noon — coffee, tea, pre-workout, anything
experiments.evidence_summary: Caffeine half-life is 5-6 hours. Consuming at 2pm means 50% is still circulating at 8pm, suppressing adenosine and reducing deep sleep.
experiments.evidence_citation: Drake et al., J Clin Sleep Med 2013
experiments.hypothesis_template: Eliminating caffeine after 12pm for {duration} days will reduce sleep latency and increase deep sleep % by ≥10%.
experiments.protocol_template: No coffee, tea, energy drinks, pre-workout, or chocolate after 12:00pm. Morning caffeine unchanged. Track sleep latency, deep sleep %, HRV.
experiments.why_it_matters: That 2pm coffee costs you 30 minutes of deep sleep tonight. Most people don't feel it — the data shows it.

experiments.name: Consistent Bedtime Routine
experiments.description: Same 3-step wind-down routine every night — same order, same timing
experiments.evidence_summary: Behavioral conditioning: consistent pre-sleep rituals create a Pavlovian sleep cue. Limited formal studies but strong clinical consensus.
experiments.evidence_citation: Mindell et al., Sleep 2009 (pediatric, mechanism applies to adults)
experiments.hypothesis_template: Following the same 3-step bedtime routine for {duration} days will establish a reliable sleep cue.
experiments.protocol_template: Choose 3 steps (e.g., tea → journal → stretch). Same order every night. Start at same time. No variation. Track compliance.
experiments.why_it_matters: Rituals aren't woo — they're conditioning. Your brain learns that step 1 means sleep is coming.

experiments.name: Breathwork Before Sleep
experiments.description: 4-7-8 breathing pattern for 5 minutes before bed
experiments.evidence_summary: Extended exhale activates parasympathetic nervous system. 4-7-8 pattern reduces heart rate and promotes relaxation.
experiments.evidence_citation: Ma et al., Frontiers in Psychology 2017
experiments.hypothesis_template: 5 minutes of 4-7-8 breathing before bed for {duration} days will increase pre-sleep HRV and reduce sleep onset latency.
experiments.protocol_template: In bed, lights off. Inhale 4 counts, hold 7 counts, exhale 8 counts. Repeat for 5 minutes. Track HRV trend and sleep onset.
experiments.why_it_matters: Free. Takes 5 minutes. The extended exhale is a direct parasympathetic lever.

experiments.name: Post-Dinner Walk
experiments.description: Walk for 10 minutes after your largest meal each day
experiments.evidence_summary: Post-meal walking reduces glucose spikes by 30-50%. Even light walking (2-3 mph) is effective. Works via skeletal muscle glucose uptake.
experiments.evidence_citation: Buffey et al., Sports Medicine 2022
experiments.hypothesis_template: Walking 10 minutes after dinner for {duration} days will reduce post-meal glucose spikes by ≥20% vs baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: Walk for minimum 10 minutes within 30 minutes of finishing the largest meal. Any pace. Track via Garmin/Strava. Measure glucose response via CGM on walk days vs non-walk baseline.
experiments.why_it_matters: The post-dinner walk is the most underrated intervention in metabolic health. Ten minutes. No gear. No app. Just gravity and glucose.

experiments.name: Morning Mobility Routine
experiments.description: 10-minute mobility routine within 30 minutes of waking
experiments.evidence_summary: Morning movement increases body temperature, cortisol awakening response, and joint synovial fluid production. Reduces injury risk in subsequent training.
experiments.evidence_citation: Behm & Chaouachi, European J Applied Physiology 2011
experiments.hypothesis_template: A consistent 10-minute morning mobility routine for {duration} days will become automatic and improve subjective readiness.
experiments.protocol_template: Within 30 min of waking: hip circles, cat-cow, world's greatest stretch, ankle circles, thoracic rotation. 10 min. Same sequence. Track compliance.
experiments.why_it_matters: The body you wake up in isn't the body you had yesterday. Ten minutes of movement reminds your joints they exist.

experiments.name: Zone 2 Cardio 3x/Week
experiments.description: Minimum 3 sessions per week of 30+ minutes at Zone 2 heart rate
experiments.evidence_summary: Zone 2 training improves mitochondrial density, fat oxidation, and VO2 max. Strongest predictor of all-cause mortality reduction.
experiments.evidence_citation: Mandsager et al., JAMA Network Open 2018
experiments.hypothesis_template: 3+ Zone 2 sessions per week for {duration} days will reduce RHR by ≥3 bpm and improve HRV by ≥8%.
experiments.protocol_template: 3 sessions/week minimum, 30 min each, at Zone 2 (MAF 180 - age, or nasal breathing pace). Walk, bike, or row. Track via Garmin HR. Measure RHR, HRV weekly trend.
experiments.why_it_matters: Cardiorespiratory fitness is the single strongest predictor of longevity. Zone 2 is where you build it.

experiments.name: Daily 8,000+ Steps
experiments.description: Hit 8,000 steps every single day, no exceptions
experiments.evidence_summary: 8,000 steps/day associated with 51% lower all-cause mortality vs 4,000. Dose-response plateaus around 12,000.
experiments.evidence_citation: Saint-Maurice et al., JAMA 2020
experiments.hypothesis_template: Hitting 8,000+ steps every day for {duration} days will improve recovery scores and support weight trend.
experiments.protocol_template: 8,000 steps minimum daily. Garmin tracks automatically. No exceptions — rain, travel, rest days all count. Walk to make up the difference if training doesn't cover it.
experiments.why_it_matters: Steps are the background radiation of fitness. They don't feel like exercise — that's the point.

experiments.name: Cold Shower Every Morning
experiments.description: 2 minutes of cold water at the end of every morning shower
experiments.evidence_summary: Cold exposure increases norepinephrine 2-3x, improves mood, and may enhance brown fat activation. HRV effects mixed but trending positive.
experiments.evidence_citation: Buijze et al., PLOS ONE 2016
experiments.hypothesis_template: Cold showers (2 min) every morning for {duration} days will increase morning HRV by ≥5% vs baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: End of morning shower: turn to coldest setting. 2 minutes minimum. Breathe normally. Track compliance, morning HRV, and recovery score.
experiments.why_it_matters: The hardest thing you do all day, done first. Two minutes of discomfort that rewires your relationship with avoidance.

experiments.name: Grip Strength Training 3x/Week
experiments.description: Dedicated grip work — dead hangs, farmer carries, or gripper sets
experiments.evidence_summary: Grip strength is independently associated with cardiovascular mortality and all-cause mortality. Each 5kg decrease in grip = 17% higher mortality.
experiments.evidence_citation: Leong et al., The Lancet 2015
experiments.hypothesis_template: Dedicated grip training 3x/week for {duration} days will increase grip strength by ≥10% vs baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: 3 sessions/week: dead hangs (3 sets max hold), farmer carries (3 x 30m), or gripper sets (3 x 10). Progressive overload. Test grip monthly with dynamometer.
experiments.why_it_matters: Grip strength predicts how long you'll live better than blood pressure does. It's also the first thing to go.

experiments.name: Stretching Before Bed
experiments.description: 10-minute gentle stretching routine before sleep
experiments.evidence_summary: Light stretching may activate parasympathetic nervous system and reduce muscle tension. Limited controlled studies on sleep quality.
experiments.evidence_citation: Banno et al., Scientific Reports 2018
experiments.hypothesis_template: 10 minutes of stretching before bed for {duration} days will improve sleep quality score.
experiments.protocol_template: 10 min gentle stretching: hamstrings, hip flexors, chest, shoulders, neck. No intense or ballistic stretching. In pajamas, low light. Track sleep score.
experiments.why_it_matters: Your body carries the tension of the day into bed. Ten minutes of unwinding is a physical signal that work is over.

experiments.name: Active Recovery Day Protocol
experiments.description: On rest days: walk + mobility + sauna (or hot bath) instead of full rest
experiments.evidence_summary: Active recovery increases blood flow and lymphatic drainage vs passive rest. Heat exposure (sauna) enhances recovery markers.
experiments.evidence_citation: Laukkanen et al., JAMA Internal Medicine 2015
experiments.hypothesis_template: Replacing passive rest days with active recovery for {duration} days will maintain recovery scores above baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: On non-training days: 30 min walk + 10 min mobility + sauna/hot bath. No lifting, no HIIT. Track recovery score trend.
experiments.why_it_matters: Rest doesn't mean stillness. The body recovers faster when it moves — just not hard.

experiments.name: 40g Protein at Breakfast
experiments.description: Hit 40g of protein at first meal every day
experiments.evidence_summary: High-protein breakfast reduces ghrelin, increases satiety hormones, and reduces total daily caloric intake by 10-15%. Supports muscle protein synthesis.
experiments.evidence_citation: Leidy et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2015
experiments.hypothesis_template: 40g protein at breakfast for {duration} days will reduce total daily caloric intake and support weight loss trajectory.
experiments.protocol_template: First meal must contain ≥40g protein (eggs, Greek yogurt, protein shake, etc.). Log in MacroFactor. Track weight trend and satiety.
experiments.why_it_matters: Front-loading protein changes the entire day. You eat less later without trying.

experiments.name: 16:8 Intermittent Fasting
experiments.description: Eat within an 8-hour window, fast for 16 hours daily
experiments.evidence_summary: Time-restricted eating improves insulin sensitivity, reduces fasting glucose, and may support autophagy. Weight loss effects primarily from caloric reduction.
experiments.evidence_citation: Wilkinson et al., Cell Metabolism 2020
experiments.hypothesis_template: 16:8 fasting for {duration} days will reduce fasting glucose by ≥5 mg/dL and support weight loss trend.
experiments.protocol_template: Eating window 12pm-8pm (adjustable). Water, black coffee, and plain tea allowed during fast. Log meal times in MacroFactor. Track fasting glucose, weight.
experiments.why_it_matters: The simplest dietary intervention — you don't change what you eat, just when. The 16-hour fast does the rest.

experiments.name: No Alcohol for 30 Days
experiments.description: Zero alcohol consumption for 30 days
experiments.evidence_summary: Even moderate alcohol disrupts sleep architecture, raises RHR, suppresses HRV, impairs glucose regulation, and adds empty calories.
experiments.evidence_citation: Colrain et al., Handbook of Clinical Neurology 2014
experiments.hypothesis_template: 30 days without alcohol will improve HRV by ≥10%, sleep score by ≥5 pts, and support weight loss.
experiments.protocol_template: Zero alcohol — beer, wine, spirits, all of it. Social situations included. Track HRV, sleep score, weight, fasting glucose, RHR daily.
experiments.why_it_matters: Alcohol is the most socially accepted compound that wrecks every biomarker we track. Thirty days reveals the real cost.

experiments.name: Fiber Target ≥30g/Day
experiments.description: Hit 30 grams of dietary fiber every day
experiments.evidence_summary: High fiber intake reduces glucose variability, improves gut microbiome diversity, and is associated with lower all-cause mortality.
experiments.evidence_citation: Reynolds et al., The Lancet 2019
experiments.hypothesis_template: Hitting ≥30g fiber daily for {duration} days will reduce glucose variability and support weight trend.
experiments.protocol_template: Track fiber in MacroFactor. Target 30g/day from whole food sources — vegetables, legumes, whole grains, berries. Supplement with psyllium if needed. Track glucose variability.
experiments.why_it_matters: Most Americans get 15g of fiber. Doubling it changes your gut, your glucose, and your satiety.

experiments.name: Pre-Meal Apple Cider Vinegar
experiments.description: 1 tbsp ACV in water 15 minutes before high-carb meals
experiments.evidence_summary: Acetic acid slows gastric emptying and may reduce post-meal glucose spikes by 20-30%. Mechanism well-understood, clinical data moderate.
experiments.evidence_citation: Shishehbor et al., Pakistan J Biological Sciences 2008
experiments.hypothesis_template: ACV before high-carb meals for {duration} days will reduce post-meal glucose peaks by ≥15%.
experiments.protocol_template: 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar in 8oz water, 15 min before meals with >50g carbs. Track post-meal glucose via CGM. Compare spike magnitude vs non-ACV meals.
experiments.why_it_matters: A tablespoon of vinegar for a 20% reduction in glucose spikes. The cost-benefit ratio is absurd.

experiments.name: Vegetable Volume Loading
experiments.description: 2 cups of non-starchy vegetables with every meal
experiments.evidence_summary: Volume-based satiety from low-calorie-density foods reduces total caloric intake without hunger. Fiber and water content drive the effect.
experiments.evidence_citation: Rolls et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2004
experiments.hypothesis_template: Adding 2 cups of vegetables to every meal for {duration} days will reduce total caloric intake and support weight loss.
experiments.protocol_template: Every meal includes ≥2 cups of non-starchy vegetables (broccoli, spinach, peppers, etc.). Eat veggies first. Log in MacroFactor. Track weight.
experiments.why_it_matters: You can't outeat two cups of broccoli. Volume is the satiety lever most people never pull.

experiments.name: Largest Meal at Lunch
experiments.description: Shift caloric distribution so lunch is the biggest meal, dinner is light
experiments.evidence_summary: Glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity are highest in the morning/afternoon. Late eating impairs glucose disposal and may disrupt sleep.
experiments.evidence_citation: Garaulet et al., International J of Obesity 2013
experiments.hypothesis_template: Eating the largest meal at lunch for {duration} days will improve fasting glucose and sleep quality.
experiments.protocol_template: Lunch = 40-50% of daily calories. Dinner = 20-30%. Track meal sizes in MacroFactor. Measure fasting glucose and sleep score.
experiments.why_it_matters: Your body processes food better when the sun is up. Dinner is tradition, not biology.

experiments.name: Hydration Target
experiments.description: Drink bodyweight/2 in ounces of water daily (e.g., 280 lbs = 140 oz)
experiments.evidence_summary: Chronic mild dehydration impairs cognitive function, mood, and exercise performance. Optimal hydration targets are debated but higher is generally better.
experiments.evidence_citation: Popkin et al., Nutrition Reviews 2010
experiments.hypothesis_template: Hitting hydration target daily for {duration} days will become habitual.
experiments.protocol_template: Calculate target: bodyweight in lbs ÷ 2 = daily oz. Track with water bottle marks or app. Spread throughout the day. Reduce 2 hours before bed.
experiments.why_it_matters: Most people are mildly dehydrated most of the time. It's the most boring intervention that actually matters.

experiments.name: Tongkat Ali — Recovery Optimization
experiments.description: 400mg daily Eurycoma longifolia extract for recovery and hormonal support
experiments.evidence_summary: May increase bioavailable testosterone and reduce cortisol. Limited but positive human trials on recovery and body composition.
experiments.evidence_citation: Talbott et al., J Int Soc Sports Nutrition 2013
experiments.hypothesis_template: 400mg daily Tongkat Ali for {duration} days will improve WHOOP recovery score by ≥5 points.
experiments.protocol_template: 400mg standardized extract daily with breakfast. No other supplement changes. Track recovery score, HRV, and subjective energy.
experiments.why_it_matters: One of the few non-prescription compounds with human trial data on testosterone and cortisol modulation.

experiments.name: NMN — NAD+ Precursor Stack
experiments.description: 250mg NMN daily for cellular energy and longevity biomarkers
experiments.evidence_summary: NMN raises intracellular NAD+ levels. Animal studies show improved metabolic function. Human trials show improved insulin sensitivity.
experiments.evidence_citation: Yoshino et al., Science 2021
experiments.hypothesis_template: 250mg NMN daily for {duration} days will produce measurable improvement in HRV (≥8%) and subjective energy.
experiments.protocol_template: 250mg NMN sublingually each morning on empty stomach. Track HRV, recovery, subjective energy. No other supplement changes.
experiments.why_it_matters: NAD+ declines with age. If you can raise it back, the downstream effects touch everything from DNA repair to mitochondrial function.

experiments.name: Creatine Monohydrate — Strength
experiments.description: 5g daily creatine monohydrate for strength progression
experiments.evidence_summary: Most studied supplement in sports science. Increases phosphocreatine stores, improves strength, power, and lean mass. Safe long-term.
experiments.evidence_citation: Kreider et al., J Int Soc Sports Nutrition 2017
experiments.hypothesis_template: 5g daily creatine for {duration} days will produce ≥8% increase in major lift 1RMs vs pre-supplementation baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: 5g creatine monohydrate daily (no loading phase). With any meal. Track major lift progression. Measure body composition.
experiments.why_it_matters: The most evidence-backed supplement in existence. If you're lifting and not taking creatine, you're leaving gains on the table.

experiments.name: Berberine — Glucose Attenuation
experiments.description: 500mg Berberine before high-carb meals for glucose management
experiments.evidence_summary: Berberine activates AMPK pathway, rivaling metformin in glucose-lowering effects. Reduces post-prandial spikes and HbA1c.
experiments.evidence_citation: Yin et al., Metabolism 2008
experiments.hypothesis_template: 500mg Berberine before high-carb meals will reduce CGM peaks above 140 mg/dL by ≥20%.
experiments.protocol_template: 500mg Berberine 20 min before high-carb meals. Track post-meal glucose via CGM. Compare with non-berberine control days.
experiments.why_it_matters: Nature's metformin. The glucose data on this compound is hard to argue with.

experiments.name: Ashwagandha — Cortisol/Stress
experiments.description: 600mg KSM-66 ashwagandha daily for stress and HRV
experiments.evidence_summary: KSM-66 reduces cortisol by 20-30% in multiple RCTs. Improves sleep quality, reduces anxiety, and may improve HRV.
experiments.evidence_citation: Chandrasekhar et al., Indian J Psychological Medicine 2012
experiments.hypothesis_template: 600mg KSM-66 ashwagandha for {duration} days will improve HRV by ≥8% and sleep score by ≥3 pts.
experiments.protocol_template: 600mg KSM-66 ashwagandha daily with dinner. No other adaptogen changes. Track HRV, sleep score, recovery, subjective stress.
experiments.why_it_matters: The most-studied adaptogen. If cortisol is the slow killer, ashwagandha is the evidence-based counter.

experiments.name: Omega-3 High Dose (3g EPA+DHA)
experiments.description: 3g combined EPA+DHA daily for inflammation and HRV
experiments.evidence_summary: High-dose omega-3s reduce triglycerides, CRP, and may improve HRV. EPA specifically linked to cardiovascular benefit.
experiments.evidence_citation: Bhatt et al., NEJM 2019 (REDUCE-IT)
experiments.hypothesis_template: 3g EPA+DHA daily for {duration} days will improve HRV and reduce RHR.
experiments.protocol_template: 3g combined EPA+DHA from high-quality fish oil. With a fat-containing meal for absorption. Track HRV, RHR. Labs at 60 days if feasible (triglycerides, CRP).
experiments.why_it_matters: The dose most people take (1g) is below therapeutic threshold. Three grams is where the evidence lives.

experiments.name: Vitamin D Optimization (5000 IU)
experiments.description: 5000 IU Vitamin D3 daily to optimize blood levels
experiments.evidence_summary: Pacific Northwest = chronic deficiency risk. Optimal levels (50-80 ng/mL) associated with immune function, mood, and bone health.
experiments.evidence_citation: Holick, NEJM 2007
experiments.hypothesis_template: 5000 IU Vitamin D3 daily for {duration} days will raise 25(OH)D level to ≥50 ng/mL.
experiments.protocol_template: 5000 IU Vitamin D3 with K2 (MK-7) daily with a fat-containing meal. Blood test at baseline and 90 days. Track mood subjectively.
experiments.why_it_matters: Living in Seattle and not supplementing Vitamin D is a guaranteed deficiency. This is the most obvious supplement for the Pacific Northwest.

experiments.name: L-Theanine for Focus
experiments.description: 200mg L-theanine with morning coffee for calm focus
experiments.evidence_summary: L-theanine promotes alpha brain waves and reduces jitteriness from caffeine. Combined with caffeine, improves attention and task switching.
experiments.evidence_citation: Owen et al., Nutritional Neuroscience 2008
experiments.hypothesis_template: 200mg L-theanine with morning coffee for {duration} days will improve subjective focus and reduce caffeine jitteriness.
experiments.protocol_template: 200mg L-theanine taken with first morning coffee. No other nootropic changes. Track subjective focus, HRV, and anxiety levels.
experiments.why_it_matters: The caffeine smoothing agent. Same energy, less edge. The combo is better than either alone.

experiments.name: Journaling Before Bed
experiments.description: 10 minutes of free-writing or structured journaling before sleep
experiments.evidence_summary: Expressive writing reduces intrusive thoughts, improves sleep onset, and may reduce cortisol. Structured gratitude journaling improves wellbeing.
experiments.evidence_citation: Pennebaker & Beall, J Abnormal Psychology 1986; Scullin et al., J Experimental Psychology 2018
experiments.hypothesis_template: Journaling for 10 minutes before bed for {duration} days will become habitual and reduce time-to-sleep.
experiments.protocol_template: 10 minutes before bed. Free-write or use the platform journal templates (state of mind, gratitude, reflection). No rules on content. Track compliance.
experiments.why_it_matters: Writing is thinking made visible. The brain that dumps its contents onto a page sleeps better than the one carrying it all to bed.

experiments.name: Reading 10 Pages a Day
experiments.description: Read 10 pages of a physical book every day
experiments.evidence_summary: Regular reading associated with reduced cognitive decline and increased lifespan. 10 pages/day = ~15 books/year.
experiments.evidence_citation: Bavishi et al., Social Science & Medicine 2016
experiments.hypothesis_template: Reading 10 pages daily for {duration} days will establish a consistent reading habit.
experiments.protocol_template: 10 pages minimum of a physical book (not phone/tablet). Any genre. Track pages and book title daily.
experiments.why_it_matters: Ten pages is nothing. That's the point. The barrier is so low that skipping feels ridiculous. Fifteen books a year changes how you think.

experiments.name: Meditation 10 Minutes Daily
experiments.description: Guided meditation for 10 minutes each morning
experiments.evidence_summary: Mindfulness meditation reduces cortisol, improves HRV, reduces anxiety symptoms, and increases gray matter density in the prefrontal cortex.
experiments.evidence_citation: Goyal et al., JAMA Internal Medicine 2014
experiments.hypothesis_template: 10 minutes of daily meditation for {duration} days will improve HRV by ≥5% and reduce subjective stress.
experiments.protocol_template: 10 minutes guided meditation (Waking Up, Headspace, or similar). Same time daily, ideally morning. Track compliance, HRV, recovery, subjective stress.
experiments.why_it_matters: The most studied mental health intervention that isn't therapy or medication. Ten minutes. Sit still. That's it.

experiments.name: Gratitude Log (3 Items/Day)
experiments.description: Write down 3 specific things you're grateful for each day
experiments.evidence_summary: Gratitude interventions improve wellbeing, reduce depressive symptoms, and improve sleep quality. Effect is dose-dependent on specificity.
experiments.evidence_citation: Emmons & McCullough, JPSP 2003
experiments.hypothesis_template: Logging 3 specific gratitudes daily for {duration} days will measurably improve mood and wellbeing scores.
experiments.protocol_template: Each evening, write 3 specific things from today (not generic). E.g., 'Brittany made me laugh during dinner' not 'grateful for family.' Platform journal or paper.
experiments.why_it_matters: The brain defaults to scanning for threats. Gratitude practice rewires it to scan for good. Specificity is the key — generic gratitude doesn't stick.

experiments.name: No News/Social Media Before 10am
experiments.description: Zero news or social media consumption before 10am
experiments.evidence_summary: Morning information consumption hijacks the cortisol awakening response and sets a reactive (vs proactive) tone for the day. Limited formal studies.
experiments.evidence_citation: Clinical consensus; Newport, Digital Minimalism 2019
experiments.hypothesis_template: No news or social media before 10am for {duration} days will improve morning focus and reduce reactive thinking.
experiments.protocol_template: No Twitter, Reddit, news apps, news sites, Instagram, YouTube, or TikTok before 10am. Phone can be used for messages, music, and navigation. Track compliance.
experiments.why_it_matters: The first thing you consume sets the frame for the day. Other people's emergencies are not yours.

experiments.name: Deep Work Block (2 Hours)
experiments.description: One uninterrupted 2-hour block of focused work per day
experiments.evidence_summary: Context-switching costs 23 minutes per interruption. Deep work blocks dramatically increase cognitive output per hour.
experiments.evidence_citation: Mark et al., CHI 2008; Newport, Deep Work 2016
experiments.hypothesis_template: A daily 2-hour deep work block for {duration} days will increase high-quality output and reduce perceived busyness.
experiments.protocol_template: Block 2 consecutive hours daily. Phone on DND. Close email/Slack. Single task only. No tab switching. Track compliance and what was accomplished.
experiments.why_it_matters: Two hours of real focus produces more than eight hours of multitasking. The problem isn't time — it's attention.

experiments.name: Learning Something New 15 Min/Day
experiments.description: Spend 15 minutes daily learning a new skill, language, or subject
experiments.evidence_summary: Novel learning stimulates neuroplasticity and cognitive reserve. Even brief daily sessions compound over time.
experiments.evidence_citation: Park et al., Psychological Science 2014
experiments.hypothesis_template: 15 minutes of daily novel learning for {duration} days will build a consistent learning habit.
experiments.protocol_template: 15 minutes daily on something outside your current expertise. Language app, instrument, online course, textbook. Same subject for the full experiment. Track compliance.
experiments.why_it_matters: The brain that stops learning starts declining. Fifteen minutes is the minimum effective dose for novelty.

experiments.name: Date Night Per Week
experiments.description: One dedicated date night per week with Brittany — no phones, no logistics talk
experiments.evidence_summary: Regular couple time is associated with relationship satisfaction and reduced conflict. Quality time (not just proximity) drives the effect.
experiments.evidence_citation: Dew & Wilcox, J Marriage & Family 2011
experiments.hypothesis_template: Weekly date nights for {duration} days will improve relationship satisfaction and become a protected ritual.
experiments.protocol_template: One evening per week: planned activity (restaurant, walk, movie, cooking together). No phones at table. No logistics or planning talk. Track compliance and subjective quality.
experiments.why_it_matters: Proximity is not connection. A scheduled date night sounds unromantic — until it becomes the best night of the week.

experiments.name: Call a Friend/Family Member Weekly
experiments.description: One real phone call (not text) to a friend or family member per week
experiments.evidence_summary: Voice communication creates stronger social bonds than text. Social connection is the #1 predictor of wellbeing and longevity.
experiments.evidence_citation: Holt-Lunstad et al., PLOS Medicine 2010; Kumar & Epley, J Experimental Psychology 2021
experiments.hypothesis_template: One phone call per week to a friend/family member for {duration} days will deepen at least 2 relationships.
experiments.protocol_template: One call per week minimum. Different person each time (or rotating list). 10+ minutes. Log in social interaction tracker. Track who and how it felt.
experiments.why_it_matters: Men are terrible at maintaining friendships. A weekly call is the minimum viable dose for not disappearing from people's lives.

experiments.name: One Meaningful Conversation Per Day
experiments.description: Have at least one conversation that goes beyond surface-level each day
experiments.evidence_summary: Substantive conversations (vs small talk) are correlated with higher wellbeing. Depth of interaction matters more than frequency.
experiments.evidence_citation: Mehl et al., Psychological Science 2010
experiments.hypothesis_template: One meaningful conversation daily for {duration} days will shift social interaction quality measurably upward.
experiments.protocol_template: Daily: one conversation that goes past 'how are you / fine.' Ask a real question. Listen. Log who and what topic. Score depth 1-5.
experiments.why_it_matters: Most days are filled with transactional words. One real exchange changes the texture of the entire day.

experiments.name: Digital-Free Dinner
experiments.description: No phones at the dinner table — for anyone
experiments.evidence_summary: Phone presence during meals reduces perceived empathy and connection quality, even when the phone isn't being used.
experiments.evidence_citation: Przybylski & Weinstein, J Social & Personal Relationships 2013
experiments.hypothesis_template: Phone-free dinners for {duration} days will improve mealtime conversation quality.
experiments.protocol_template: All phones go face-down in another room during dinner. Everyone at the table. No exceptions. Track compliance and conversation quality subjectively.
experiments.why_it_matters: The phone on the table is a third person in every conversation. Remove it and watch what happens.

experiments.name: Weekly Appreciation Message
experiments.description: Send one genuine message of appreciation to someone each week
experiments.evidence_summary: Expressing gratitude strengthens social bonds and increases wellbeing for both sender and receiver. People consistently underestimate how much recipients value it.
experiments.evidence_citation: Kumar & Epley, Psychological Science 2018
experiments.hypothesis_template: Sending one appreciation message per week for {duration} days will strengthen at least 3 relationships.
experiments.protocol_template: Once per week: write a genuine message (text, email, or handwritten) to someone specific about something specific. Not generic praise. Log recipient and content summary.
experiments.why_it_matters: Most people never hear that they matter to you. The message you think about sending but don't — send that one.

experiments.name: Community Event Once Per Month
experiments.description: Attend one community event, meetup, or group activity per month
experiments.evidence_summary: Group belonging is a distinct predictor of wellbeing beyond individual friendships. Dunbar's research shows community-layer connections matter.
experiments.evidence_citation: Dunbar, How Many Friends Does One Person Need? 2010
experiments.hypothesis_template: Attending one community event per month for {duration} days will expand the outer social ring by ≥3 acquaintances.
experiments.protocol_template: One event per month: meetup, class, volunteer, sports league, church, anything with strangers/acquaintances. Log the event and anyone met. Track social dashboard outer ring.
experiments.why_it_matters: Male loneliness is an epidemic. The cure isn't more texting — it's showing up in a room with other people.

experiments.name: Make Bed Immediately on Waking
experiments.description: Make your bed within 2 minutes of getting up — no exceptions
experiments.evidence_summary: Keystone habit: small wins in the morning cascade into better decisions throughout the day. No direct biomarker evidence, strong behavioral theory.
experiments.evidence_citation: Duhigg, The Power of Habit 2012; McRaven, Make Your Bed 2017
experiments.hypothesis_template: Making the bed immediately on waking for {duration} days will establish a keystone habit.
experiments.protocol_template: Feet hit floor → make bed. Within 2 minutes. No phone first. Track compliance in Habitify.
experiments.why_it_matters: The smallest possible win. If you can't do this, nothing else in the system matters. If you can, everything else gets easier.

experiments.name: Evening Shutdown Routine
experiments.description: Same 5-step end-of-day ritual every evening
experiments.evidence_summary: Shutdown rituals create psychological closure between work and rest. Reduces rumination and improves next-day focus.
experiments.evidence_citation: Newport, Deep Work 2016; Smit & Barber, J Occupational Health Psychology 2016
experiments.hypothesis_template: A consistent 5-step shutdown routine for {duration} days will create clear work/rest boundaries.
experiments.protocol_template: 5 steps in order (e.g., 1. Review tomorrow's calendar 2. Close all tabs 3. Write tomorrow's top 3 4. Set out clothes 5. Say 'shutdown complete'). Track compliance.
experiments.why_it_matters: Work bleeds into rest because there's no boundary. A ritual draws the line your brain won't draw by itself.

experiments.name: Single-Tasking Blocks
experiments.description: No tab switching during focused work blocks — one task, one window
experiments.evidence_summary: Multitasking reduces performance on all tasks by 20-40%. Single-tasking is a learnable discipline with compounding benefits.
experiments.evidence_citation: Ophir et al., PNAS 2009
experiments.hypothesis_template: Single-tasking blocks for {duration} days will reduce context-switching and improve work satisfaction.
experiments.protocol_template: During focused work: one browser tab, one application. No switching until the timer ends. Use a timer (25 or 50 min). Track blocks completed.
experiments.why_it_matters: Tab switching is the fidgeting of the knowledge worker. Training yourself out of it is a competitive advantage.

experiments.name: Vice Elimination Sprint
experiments.description: Pick one vice and eliminate it completely for 30 days
experiments.evidence_summary: Abstinence from a specific vice builds self-regulation capacity and reveals dependency patterns. The clarity from removal is often more informative than the removal itself.
experiments.evidence_citation: Baumeister & Tierney, Willpower 2011
experiments.hypothesis_template: Eliminating one vice completely for {duration} days will extend the vice streak and reveal dependency patterns.
experiments.protocol_template: Choose one vice (sugar, alcohol, social media, late-night snacking, etc.). Zero tolerance for 30 days. Log temptation moments. Track vice streak in platform.
experiments.why_it_matters: You don't know what something costs you until you stop paying. Pick the vice you're most afraid to quit — that's the one.

experiments.name: Box Breathing 5 Minutes Daily
experiments.description: 5 minutes of box breathing (4-4-4-4) every morning
experiments.evidence_summary: Box breathing reduces cortisol, increases HRV, and improves autonomic nervous system regulation. Used by Navy SEALs for stress inoculation.
experiments.evidence_citation: Zaccaro et al., Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2018
experiments.hypothesis_template: 5 minutes of box breathing daily for {duration} days will increase morning HRV by ≥5%.
experiments.protocol_template: Every morning: sit, close eyes. Inhale 4 counts, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4. Repeat for 5 minutes. Track compliance, morning HRV.
experiments.why_it_matters: Five minutes. No equipment. Direct parasympathetic lever. The simplest measurable intervention for stress.

experiments.name: Cold Exposure + Breathwork Combo
experiments.description: 2 min cold shower followed immediately by 3 min breathwork
experiments.evidence_summary: Combined cold-breathwork protocol may enhance norepinephrine response and HRV recovery beyond either alone. Popular in Wim Hof methodology.
experiments.evidence_citation: Kox et al., PNAS 2014
experiments.hypothesis_template: Daily cold+breathwork for {duration} days will improve HRV and recovery scores beyond baseline.
experiments.protocol_template: Morning: 2 min cold shower (coldest setting) → immediately sit and do 3 min box breathing or Wim Hof rounds. Track HRV, recovery, compliance.
experiments.why_it_matters: The hardest 5 minutes of the day. Cold wakes the body up. Breathwork calms it back down. The combination teaches regulation under stress.

experiments.name: Track Everything for 7 Days
experiments.description: Perfect compliance on all tracking systems for 7 consecutive days
experiments.evidence_summary: Self-monitoring is the strongest behavioral predictor of weight loss and habit formation success. Perfect compliance creates a reference standard.
experiments.evidence_citation: Burke et al., J American Dietetic Association 2011
experiments.hypothesis_template: 7 days of perfect tracking compliance will establish a personal best and reveal friction points.
experiments.protocol_template: For 7 days: log every meal in MacroFactor, complete all Habitify habits, write journal entry, track supplements, weigh in, and let all wearables sync. Zero misses. Track compliance rate.
experiments.why_it_matters: Seven days of perfect data is the diagnostic tool. It shows you where the system works and where it breaks.

experiments.name: Sauna 2x/Week for 6 Weeks
experiments.description: Regular sauna use to test cardiovascular and recovery benefits
experiments.evidence_summary: 4-7 sauna sessions/week associated with 40% reduction in all-cause mortality
experiments.evidence_citation: Laukkanen et al., JAMA Internal Medicine, 2015
experiments.hypothesis_template: Regular sauna use (2x/week, 20 min at 174°F) will improve HRV by >5% and reduce resting heart rate
experiments.protocol_template: 20 minutes at 174°F (79°C), 2x per week. Hydrate before and after. No cold plunge after (isolate variable).

experiments.name: Cold Plunge 2 min, 3x/Week
experiments.description: Deliberate cold exposure for dopamine and mood
experiments.evidence_summary: Cold water immersion increases dopamine 250%, norepinephrine 530%
experiments.evidence_citation: Sramek et al., 2000; Huberman Lab
experiments.hypothesis_template: Cold plunge (2 min, 3x/week) will increase morning HRV and improve subjective mood scores
experiments.protocol_template: 2 minutes in cold water (50-59°F), 3x per week. Morning preferred. No warm shower after for 20 min.

experiments.name: Zone 2 Cardio 150+ min/Week
experiments.description: Sustained Zone 2 base building for 8 weeks
experiments.evidence_summary: Zone 2 training is the highest-evidence longevity modality — VO2max, mitochondrial density, fat oxidation
experiments.evidence_citation: Attia, Outlive; WHO guidelines
experiments.hypothesis_template: 150+ min/week Zone 2 for 8 weeks will improve cardiac efficiency (pace-at-HR) by >5%
experiments.protocol_template: Minimum 150 minutes per week in Zone 2 (60-70% max HR). Walking, cycling, or rucking. Track via Garmin/Strava HR.

experiments.name: Morning Sunlight + Evening Blue Blockers
experiments.description: Full circadian protocol: morning light + evening protection
experiments.evidence_summary: Morning light advances circadian phase; evening blue blockers preserve melatonin onset
experiments.evidence_citation: Huberman Lab; Chang et al., PNAS 2015
experiments.hypothesis_template: Combined morning sunlight + evening blue blockers will reduce sleep onset latency and increase deep sleep %
experiments.protocol_template: 10+ min outdoor light within 30 min of waking (no sunglasses). Blue-blocking glasses from sunset. Track with Whoop/Eight Sleep.

experiments.name: Time-Restricted Eating (12pm-8pm)
experiments.description: 8-hour eating window for metabolic benefits
experiments.evidence_summary: TRF improves insulin sensitivity, reduces inflammation, supports circadian alignment
experiments.evidence_citation: Panda, The Circadian Code; Sutton et al., Cell Metabolism 2018
experiments.hypothesis_template: 8-hour eating window (12pm-8pm) will reduce fasting glucose variability and improve time-in-range
experiments.protocol_template: First meal at 12pm, last bite by 8pm. Black coffee/tea/water only before noon. Track via CGM + MacroFactor.

experiments.name: Eliminate Alcohol 30 Days
experiments.description: Complete alcohol elimination to measure sleep and recovery impact
experiments.evidence_summary: Even moderate alcohol disrupts REM sleep, suppresses HRV, impairs recovery for 3+ days per drink
experiments.evidence_citation: Attia, Outlive; Huberman Lab; Walker, Why We Sleep
experiments.hypothesis_template: 30 days zero alcohol will improve REM % by >10% and HRV by >5ms
experiments.protocol_template: Zero alcohol for 30 days. No exceptions. Track REM %, HRV, recovery score, sleep efficiency via Whoop.
